coaching.coaching: Plan how verification command for applier.py stale re-exports will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: verification command for applier.py stale re-exports

coaching.coaching: Record an ADR for single canonical scope list authority — it reads like a one-way door.
coaching.subject: single canonical scope list authority

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `outbound_comments.py` will expose an injectable sanitizer/inbound-mapper seam (analogous to `outbound_differ.py`'s `outbound_mapper=None` / `inbound_mapper=None` injection parameters) so the module remains testable under `spec_from_file_location` without a live backend config.
evidence: outbound_comments.py docstring lines 18-23: 'Like outbound_differ, this module keeps its OWN lazy _load_adf / _load_comment_limits loaders (the reconciler modules are spec-loaded under test, where `from . import` does not resolve)'
outbound_differ.py:441-451: uses an injection parameter (outbound_mapper=None) with a fallback to select_backend — the proven pattern that handles the spec-load isolation
Plan: 'outbound_comments.py resolves the configured backend's sanitizer via the registry seam (select_backend(load_config()))' — no mention of an injection parameter or spec-load guard
findings.finding: The plan asserts `outbound_comments.py` will resolve both sanitizer and inbound mapper via `select_backend(load_config())`. The module's own docstring explicitly states it uses 'its OWN lazy `_load_adf` / `_load_comment_limits` loaders (the reconciler modules are spec-loaded under test, where `from . import` does not resolve)'. The plan does not address how `select_backend(load_config())` — which requires `rebar.config` and the backend registry — will work when `outbound_comments.py` is spec-loaded as a standalone module in tests, nor does it specify an injection seam (like the `inbound_mapper=None` fallback in `outbound_differ.py:441`) for `outbound_comments`.
scenarios: Existing unit tests that spec_from_file_location-load outbound_comments.py will fail if select_backend(load_config()) is called at import time or without a config seam
The outbound-comment ADF decode test called out in the new Added AC requires an injectable InboundMapper — without an injection seam the test must configure a full backend

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command (or test assertion) that explicitly verifies `_ADF_KEY_APPLIER`, `_AdfModule_Applier`, and `_load_adf_module` are absent from `applier.__all__` — e.g. a test asserting `all(name not in vars(applier) for name in ['_ADF_KEY_APPLIER', '_AdfModule_Applier', '_load_adf_module'])` or a `grep -c` on the `__all__` block.
evidence: '`apply_inbound_records.py`'s three call sites behave identically (ADF dict -> text) — verified by the behavior tests plus a green import of both modules'
A green import of `applier` only fails if the `import` statement references a deleted name; `__all__` with stale entries does not raise on import
No described test performs `from applier import _ADF_KEY_APPLIER` (which WOULD fail) or asserts `_ADF_KEY_APPLIER not in vars(applier_module)`
findings.finding: The added AC for `applier.py` cleanup ('applier.py no longer imports/re-exports `_ADF_KEY_APPLIER`/`_AdfModule_Applier`/`_load_adf_module`') has no proving command that would catch stale entries left in `applier.__all__`. A 'green import of both modules' check will NOT detect dead names lingering in `__all__` — Python does not raise an ImportError for `__all__` entries that name non-existent attributes until a `from applier import *` is attempted; the behavior tests and the named import check both pass silently even if the three names remain in `__all__`.
findings.location: Plan-review remediation section, Added acceptance criteria — second bullet: '`applier.py` no longer imports/re-exports `_ADF_KEY_APPLIER`/`_AdfModule_Applier`/`_load_adf_module`'
scenarios: An agent removes the three names from `inbound_translate.py` but forgets to prune `applier.__all__`; all described tests pass; the cleanup deliverable is silently incomplete
A future consumer does `from applier import *` and receives stale public names
findings.suggested_fix: Add a test case (alongside the existing sweep in `test_backend_neutrality.py` or in a new `test_applier_public_api.py`) that imports `applier` and asserts the three deleted names are absent from both `applier.__all__` and `vars(applier)`: `assert '_ADF_KEY_APPLIER' not in applier.__all__`, etc. This is runnable under `make test` and will turn red if the cleanup is missed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen the applier.py AC's verify step to assert that `_ADF_KEY_APPLIER`, `_AdfModule_Applier`, and `_load_adf_module` are NOT present in `applier.__all__` (e.g. via an import-inspection assertion in the neutrality test or a dedicated unit test).
evidence: 'verified by the behavior tests plus a green import of both modules' — a green import verifies importability, not absence of specific names from __all__
The three deleted names are not tested for absence; a refactor that accidentally re-introduces them would not be caught by this verification
findings.finding: The added AC for applier.py re-export removal states it is 'verified by the behavior tests plus a green import of both modules', but a green import does not verify that _ADF_KEY_APPLIER, _AdfModule_Applier, and _load_adf_module are absent from applier.py's __all__ and import list — it only confirms the module loads without error.
findings.location: Plan-review remediation, added acceptance criteria: 'applier.py no longer imports/re-exports _ADF_KEY_APPLIER/_AdfModule_Applier/_load_adf_module; apply_inbound_records.py's three call sites behave identically (ADF dict -> text) — verified by the behavior tests plus a green import of both modules'
scenarios: A future regression re-adds one of the three deleted names to applier.py's __all__; no test fails because the AC only requires a green import.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the backend-neutrality test (or a new unit test): `import applier; assert '_ADF_KEY_APPLIER' not in dir(applier) and '_ADF_KEY_APPLIER' not in applier.__all__` (and similarly for the other two names).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Promote `applier.py` (`src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/applier.py`) from the remediation addendum into the main **Scope** section so it is unambiguously an edit target alongside the other six files.
evidence: Main Scope section lists six files; `applier.py` is not among them.
Remediation item 3 states: 'Scope additions: src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/applier.py (drop the three dead re-exports; keep _normalize_adf_body)'.

`applier.py:111,119,124` imports `_ADF_KEY_APPLIER`, `_AdfModule_Applier`, `_load_adf_module`; `applier.py:322,326,361` lists all three in `__all__` — both must be updated when `inbound_translate.py` deletes those symbols.
findings.finding: `applier.py` is a confirmed edit target (must drop `_ADF_KEY_APPLIER`, `_AdfModule_Applier`, `_load_adf_module` from its import and `__all__`) but is absent from the plan's main **Scope** section — it appears only in the remediation addendum's 'Scope additions' line, making it invisible to a reader who treats the Scope section as authoritative.
findings.location: Scope section (main file list) vs. Plan-review remediation item 3
scenarios: An implementer working from the Scope section misses `applier.py`, leaves the three dead imports in place, and gets an `ImportError` at runtime when `inbound_translate._ADF_KEY_APPLIER` no longer exists.
A reviewer checking scope completeness flags `applier.py` edits as out-of-scope drift.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/applier.py (drop _ADF_KEY_APPLIER, _AdfModule_Applier, _load_adf_module from import and __all__; keep _normalize_adf_body)` as a seventh bullet in the main Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Merge the remediation 'Scope additions' entry for `applier.py` into the main Scope section so there is a single authoritative file list.
evidence: Main Scope section lists 7 files; applier.py is absent
Remediation section adds 'Scope additions: src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/applier.py (drop the three dead re-exports; keep _normalize_adf_body)' as a separate bullet
The two scope definitions are not reconciled into a single authoritative list
findings.finding: The main Scope section and the remediation 'Scope additions' section define scope in two separate lists, with applier.py appearing only in the remediation section. An implementer reading the Scope section alone would not know applier.py is in scope, creating a coherence gap between plan sections.
findings.location: Scope section vs. Plan-review remediation section ('Scope additions: src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/applier.py')
scenarios: An implementer working from the Scope section omits the applier.py changes, leaving dead re-exports in place and breaking the sweep-gate AC.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/applier.py (drop _ADF_KEY_APPLIER, _AdfModule_Applier, _load_adf_module from imports and __all__; keep _normalize_adf_body)` to the main Scope bullet list and remove the duplicate entry from the remediation section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update all existing `InboundMapper` test fakes (including `_RecordingInboundMapper` in `test_backend_neutrality.py`) to implement `normalize_rich_text(body: Any) -> str` after it is added to the `InboundMapper` Protocol, and add this to the plan's scope/testing sections.
evidence: Plan: 'InboundMapper gains normalize_rich_text(body: Any) -> str'
_backend.py:140-146: InboundMapper Protocol currently has only map_remote_to_local
test_backend_neutrality.py:245-253: _RecordingInboundMapper.map_remote_to_local is the only method; no normalize_rich_text
Plan scope additions: 'applier.py (drop the three dead re-exports)' — test fake updates are not mentioned
If outbound_comments routes through select_backend().inbound.normalize_rich_text and the test uses _RecordingInboundMapper, AttributeError at runtime
findings.finding: The plan adds `normalize_rich_text` to the `InboundMapper` Protocol in `_backend.py`, and routes both `inbound_translate._normalize_adf_body` and `outbound_comments._normalize_comment_body` through it. However, the existing `_RecordingInboundMapper` fake in `test_backend_neutrality.py` (lines 245-253) only implements `map_remote_to_local` and has no `normalize_rich_text`. If `normalize_rich_text` is called on that fake (e.g., via the outbound-differ or inbound-translate call sites under test), tests will fail at runtime with AttributeError. The plan's scope additions name `test_backend_neutrality.py` only for the new sweep test, not for updating existing fakes.
scenarios: Any test in test_backend_neutrality.py that exercises the outbound-comment or inbound-translate path via the _RecordingInboundMapper fake will hit AttributeError on normalize_rich_text
Other test modules with InboundMapper fakes (e.g., ad-hoc dicts or objects) similarly lack normalize_rich_text
findings.suggested_fix: Add `def normalize_rich_text(self, body): return body if isinstance(body, str) else str(body)` to `_RecordingInboundMapper` and audit other test fakes for the same gap; add 'update InboundMapper test fakes' to Scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (e.g., 'revert commits X–Y; the old lazy-key paths are self-contained and require no data migration') or describe an expand-contract deployment sequence that keeps the old keys alive until consumers are confirmed clean.
evidence: The plan justifies deletions as dead code/vendor reach-through but never describes a rollback path: no expand-contract sequencing, no feature flag, no 'to revert, do X'
'Rejected alternatives' and remediation sections argue for correctness of deletion but omit reversibility
T4 criterion: 'The remedy for a destructive/breaking change is an explicit ROLLBACK / back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing — acknowledgement alone is insufficient'
findings.finding: The plan performs destructive deletions (two lazy-key literals from outbound_comments.py, one from inbound_translate.py, and three re-exports from applier.py) with no explicit rollback or back-out plan described. T4 requires the reversibility mechanism, not merely justification of the change.
findings.location: Approach section; Plan-review remediation section; Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: A runtime issue discovered after deployment (e.g., an undiscovered consumer of one of the deleted lazy-key literals in a plugin or script outside the scanned modules) requires rapid rollback; with no documented back-out plan, the team must improvise.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection: 'These changes are pure code deletions with no schema or data migration. Back-out = git revert of the feature branch. The deleted lazy-key literals are not part of any public API contract; the only consumers are the two core modules being refactored, verified by the neutrality sweep before merge.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add protocol-contract documentation for the two new port methods (`FieldSanitizer.fit_comment` — pure fit, no warning; `InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text` — dict→text, str→identity) to `_backend.py` docstrings and/or AGENTS.md so future adapter authors have a canonical implementation contract.
evidence: Plan describes FieldSanitizer gaining `fit_comment(body: str) -> str` and InboundMapper gaining `normalize_rich_text(body: Any) -> str` as new protocol members
Plan mentions `_backend.py` docstring only as existing context (`already pinned in _backend.py as the vendor hard-limit defense`) — no update to it is scoped
Scope list does not include AGENTS.md, an ADR, or any design-doc file
findings.finding: The plan adds two new methods to existing port Protocol roles (`FieldSanitizer.fit_comment` and `InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text`) but does not call for updating `_backend.py`'s docstring, AGENTS.md, or any design doc to document the expanded Protocol contracts — leaving future adapter authors without a canonical record of what the port now requires.
scenarios: A second backend author implementing these protocols for a non-Jira backend has no authoritative contract doc specifying what `fit_comment` vs `sanitize_comment` semantics differ (no-warning vs warning), or what `normalize_rich_text` must guarantee for non-dict inputs.
findings.suggested_fix: In `_backend.py`, add docstrings to both new abstract methods specifying: for `fit_comment` — 'Pure truncation to backend comment limit; MUST NOT emit warnings (compare-path only)'; for `normalize_rich_text` — 'Decode vendor rich-text to plain str; for plain str inputs, return unchanged (identity)'. Optionally add a one-line entry in AGENTS.md under the port-roles section noting the two new members and their invariants.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `fit_comment` has ≥2 additional planned call sites beyond `outbound_comments.py:324`, OR document why a single-call-site Protocol method extension is justified here despite the plan's own Rule-of-Three rationale used to reject the sixth role (e.g., the send-path `sanitize_comment` cannot substitute because the warning MUST be suppressed and there is no per-call suppression mechanism).
evidence: Plan states `fit_comment` serves 'the comment-DIFF comparison path (outbound_comments.py:324)' — one call site cited explicitly.
Codebase search for `fit_comment` returns zero matches, confirming it does not yet exist and has no other consumers.

The plan's own YAGNI argument for rejecting a sixth Protocol role ('fewer than 3 call sites for a new role') applies equally to a single-call-site method extension: every concrete backend (Jira today, any future backend) must implement `fit_comment` to satisfy the Protocol, even though only one core call site drives it.
The plan explicitly rejects a sixth role for <3 call-sites but accepts `fit_comment` at 1 call-site without addressing the asymmetry.
findings.finding: `FieldSanitizer.fit_comment` is a new Protocol method with only one identified call site (`outbound_comments.py:324`), which is below the Rule-of-Three threshold for a new abstraction and extends the contract all `FieldSanitizer` implementors must satisfy.
findings.location: Approach section — 'FieldSanitizer gains ONE new method, fit_comment(body: str) -> str'; Scope — `_backend.py`; Remediation AC — 'fit_comment is the single, consistently-named comment-fit surface'
scenarios: A future Linear/GitHub backend must implement `fit_comment` as a no-op (or backend-appropriate truncation) despite no other core consumer ever calling it — dead Protocol surface.

If the compare path at :324 were also routed through `sanitize_comment` (suppress the warning at call-site level or via an argument), `fit_comment` becomes unnecessary and the Protocol stays at its current 4-method surface.
findings.suggested_fix: If the only distinction from `sanitize_comment` is suppressing the warning, consider whether `sanitize_comment` could accept an optional `warn: bool = True` kwarg, keeping the Protocol surface flat. If the semantic distinction is load-bearing (compare path must never emit send-side warnings regardless of caller context), document that explicitly and note any other planned call sites to bring the count above 1.

findings.finding: The Approach section states FieldSanitizer gains 'fit-to-limit + marker-preserving truncation semantics currently provided by adapters/jira/comment_limits.py AND adapters/jira/adf.py', attributing adf.py's decode role to FieldSanitizer; but the Plan-review remediation section contradicts this by assigning the adf.py DECODE usage in outbound_comments.py:134 to InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text and reserving FieldSanitizer.fit_comment for pure fit-to-limit only.
findings.location: Approach section (FieldSanitizer paragraph) vs Plan-review remediation section (item 1)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, replace 'fit-to-limit + marker-preserving truncation semantics currently provided by adapters/jira/comment_limits.py AND adapters/jira/adf.py' with 'fit-to-limit + marker-preserving truncation semantics currently provided by adapters/jira/comment_limits.py (the adf.py decode role is covered by InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text, not FieldSanitizer)'.'

findings.finding: The plan's remediation section names `FieldSanitizer.fit_comment(body)` as the method that will serve the `outbound_comments.py:324` compare-body truncation path, but no such method exists in the `FieldSanitizer` Protocol in `_backend.py` (which defines `sanitize_comment`), and `fit_comment` does not appear anywhere in the codebase. The Approach section says `FieldSanitizer` 'gains the comment-fit surface' without naming a method, and the existing `sanitize_comment` already delegates to `truncate_comment_body` (the same function `outbound_comments.py:324` currently calls). It is unresolved whether the plan intends to add a NEW `fit_comment` method to the Protocol, rename `sanitize_comment` to `fit_comment`, or simply reuse the existing `sanitize_comment`.
findings.location: Plan-review remediation section, item 1 ('Outbound-comment ADF DECODE'); Approach section ('FieldSanitizer … gains the comment-fit surface'); _backend.py FieldSanitizer Protocol
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'FieldSanitizer.fit_comment(body)' in the remediation section with 'FieldSanitizer.sanitize_comment(body)' (the existing Protocol method), since _JiraSanitizer.sanitize_comment already delegates to truncate_comment_body — matching exactly what the plan requires for the :324 path. No new Protocol method is needed.

findings.finding: The Approach section names the new FieldSanitizer method `sanitize_comment(body: str) -> str` (describing it as 'already pinned'), while the Plan-review remediation section names a different method `fit_comment(body)` as the surface for the :324 compare-body truncation — leaving it unclear whether fit_comment is a new method alongside sanitize_comment, a rename, or the same method referenced by two names.
findings.location: Approach section (FieldSanitizer paragraph) vs Plan-review remediation section item 1

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
